main_summary.summary: Elbert "Big Man" Howard, a co-founder of the Black Panther Party, was interviewed in Santa Rosa, California on June 30, 2016 by David Klein for the Civil Rights History Project. Born in 1938 in Tennessee and raised by his mother and aunts in segregated schools, Howard joined the Air Force in 1956, served four years including a posting in Europe, and discharged into Oakland. While studying at Merritt College on the GI Bill he met Bobby Seale and Huey Newton, helped develop the Ten Point Platform and Program, and became the founding editor of The Black Panther newspaper. He recounts the Denzil Dowell case in Richmond, the mimeographed first issue, securing printing through Max Scherr at the Berkeley Barb, Emory Douglas's artwork, and Richard Aoki's Asian-community organizing. Howard later served as deputy minister of information, traveled to Japan, Sweden, Denmark, and Germany on Party business, and worked on legal defense for jailed Panthers, including the Bobby Seale and Ericka Huggins New Haven trial and the Attica prison negotiations. He describes Sam Napier's murder in New York, his own jailing in the Tombs, leaving the Party after Huey Newton dissolved the chapters, and later co-founding PACH in Sonoma County.
key_themes: Founding of the Black Panther Party
The Black Panther newspaper and Party media
Police accountability and armed community patrols
Legal defense and political prisoner support
main_summary.historical_significance:
Howard is one of the six original founding members of the Black Panther Party and the founding editor of its newspaper, giving this interview unusual authority on the Party's Oakland origin period (1966-1967), the development of the Ten Point Platform, and the build-out of the newspaper from a single mimeographed sheet on the Denzil Dowell case to a weekly with reported print runs of 250,000. The interview is also a primary-source account of the Party's international outreach (Japan, Sweden, Denmark, Germany) and of specific high-profile defense episodes, the New Haven trial of Bobby Seale and Ericka Huggins, the Attica negotiations, and the Sam Napier killing during the East Coast-West Coast schism.

Howard's testimony about COINTELPRO tactics, including FBI agents pressuring his elderly aunt and the Freedom of Information Act file he later received, complements documentary records of federal surveillance of the Party. His account of the Merritt College parolee work-study program and the late-1970s decision to leave the organization following Huey Newton's directive to consolidate chapters in Oakland adds first-person framing to the Party's institutional decline. The closing material on his Sonoma County police-accountability work (PACH) and the Andy Lopez shooting links the Panther organizing model directly to ongoing 21st-century activism.

chapters.part: From Tennessee to the Air Force
chapters.title: Big Man of the Black Panther Party
chapters.summary: Interviewed by David Cline in Santa Rosa, California, in June 2016, with John Bishop on camera and Guha Shankar of the Library of Congress present, Elbert "Big Man" Howard introduces himself as a native of Chattanooga, Tennessee, born in 1938, who settled in Northern California after four years in the Air Force and made his way to Oakland and Merritt College.

chapters.part: From Tennessee to the Air Force
chapters.title: Raised by Women in a Segregated South
chapters.summary: Howard describes a fatherless childhood in Tennessee, raised as an only son by his mother and her sisters with his cousins as brothers and sisters, attending all-segregated schools, hearing talk of integration around 1954, and crediting teachers who tried to prepare him to survive in a hostile world before he left after the eleventh grade.

chapters.part: From Tennessee to the Air Force
chapters.title: Stay Away From the Army
chapters.summary: To support his single mother and develop himself, Howard joined the Air Force in 1956, a few years after the Korean War, after picking the brains of friends and relatives who warned him away from the Army as the cattle chute into combat, choosing instead a service that worked like a job with meals, lodging, and freedom to explore.

chapters.part: From Tennessee to the Air Force
chapters.title: A Revelation in Paris and Stockholm
chapters.summary: Howard recalls being sent to Europe, getting to know Paris, Stockholm, and Copenhagen, meeting people of other cultures, and realizing the white people he met were nothing like the whites he had feared in the South, an awakening into wine, art museums, and conversation that he counts as a turning point in his maturity.

chapters.part: From Tennessee to the Air Force
chapters.title: Looked Over and Screwed Over
chapters.summary: Howard reflects on the nuances of segregation inside the Air Force, even in France, where Black servicemen stuck together for cultural kinship and got passed over for promotions and economic advancement, weighing whether to make a career of it, and traces his own musical progression from spirituals and blues to R&B and bebop while jazz musicians who had fled the United States played the clubs of Europe.

chapters.part: Oakland and the Birth of an Idea
chapters.title: Taking the Discharge in Oakland
chapters.summary: Stationed at an air base near Oakland and drawn to the city's blues, jazz, soul food, and friendly people who took him under their wing, Howard chose to take his discharge in Northern California, found jobs scarce, and used his GI Bill to enroll at the city college in Oakland to sustain himself while he looked for work.

chapters.part: Oakland and the Birth of an Idea
chapters.title: Rap Sessions at Merritt College
chapters.summary: As he studied Black history and tried to develop his writing, Howard met Bobby Seale and Huey Newton at Merritt College, and in rap sessions after class they discussed Oakland police repression and people being killed by cops, searching for a new approach beyond civil rights and nonviolence, a progressive and revolutionary path embodied in the ten-point platform of a new organization.

chapters.part: Oakland and the Birth of an Idea
chapters.title: Cherry-Picking the Revolutionaries
chapters.summary: Howard explains that the party's first demand was the power to determine their own destiny, that they saw their community occupied by police doing the bidding of rich politicians, and that they studied Che Guevara, Mao Zedong, Kwame Nkrumah, and Malcolm X, cherry-picking philosophies to build their own approach, knowing they were not in China or Cuba.

chapters.part: Oakland and the Birth of an Idea
chapters.title: A Listener Who Filed It Away
chapters.summary: Howard describes hanging more with Bobby Seale than Huey Newton, going to Seale for advice on the justice system and his own run-ins with police, admiring Seale's skill for organizing and his far-out ideas, and recalling that Newton had grown up after his family's time in a relocation camp and came to live among the poor and oppressed Black people of Oakland.

chapters.part: The Ticket, the Shotgun, and the Patrols
chapters.title: A Brand New Red Pickup Truck
chapters.summary: Howard tells of leaving a club run by former basketball player Don Barksdale on a drizzly night, pulling his new red pickup to the front to collect his date, and being singled out for a ticket while the cops skipped the cars behind him, protesting, cursing, driving off, and being boxed in and hauled to jail, the incident that pushed him to get involved.

chapters.part: The Ticket, the Shotgun, and the Patrols
chapters.title: A Legal Shotgun in the Rack
chapters.summary: Howard recounts going to court with Huey Newton's advice, the judge dismissing the ticket while saying he was probably guilty as the cops laughed in the courtroom, and how, left highly angry, he mounted a rack in his pickup with a legal twelve-gauge shotgun, having confirmed with Newton on a ride home that a weapon openly carried in the car was perfectly legal.

chapters.part: The Ticket, the Shotgun, and the Patrols
chapters.title: Observing the Cops on Patrol
chapters.summary: Howard describes how, as membership and firearms grew and Newton confirmed the law, the party patrolled their community at night and on weekends, observing officers performing their duty so they would not abuse people, advising those arrested not to resist but to let the Panthers follow to the courthouse and bail them out, which drew curious recruits into political education classes rather than shootouts.

chapters.title: He Couldn't Possibly Jump That Fence
chapters.summary: Howard recounts the Denzil Dowell case in Richmond, where authorities told the family they were justified in shooting Dowell in the back as he climbed a fence, his mother insisting an injury made that impossible, and how the party exposed the lie, held a demonstration at the Richmond courthouse, and produced a mimeographed sheet explaining what really happened.

chapters.title: The Symbolic Power of the Gun
chapters.summary: Howard reflects on showing up armed at the Richmond rally so the community saw the party was ready to defend itself and them, a demonstrated determination that drew people without fear, and the growing flood of requests for support as bunches of people showed up with enthusiasm whenever the party called a rally.

chapters.title: We Need Our Own Organ
chapters.summary: Howard explains the need to rent an office so the party had a focus in the community rather than being a roaming group of young men with guns, and tells how, after Bobby Seale said the party needed its own news organ, the first mimeographed sheet with Howard writing the text and Seale drawing the panther graphic became their news outlet, with Seale naming him the paper's first editor.

chapters.title: Minister of Information
chapters.summary: Howard recounts that when Eldridge Cleaver, best-selling author of Soul on Ice, got out of prison and became editor, Howard was made deputy minister of information, charged with putting out a steady stream of public information through pamphlets, and notes he gave little weight to titles, wanting only to do the work and put a fork in the system.

chapters.part: Vital Organ: The Black Panther Newspaper
chapters.title: Combating the Oakland Tribune
chapters.summary: Howard frames the newspaper as vital to combating the negative coverage in the Oakland Tribune and the San Francisco Chronicle by telling the community the truth about incidents and issues, and describes requiring chapters across the country to send in news and pictures to Oakland headquarters, which he edited and printed for nationwide distribution.

chapters.part: Vital Organ: The Black Panther Newspaper
chapters.title: Two Hundred Fifty Thousand Copies a Week
chapters.summary: Howard recalls the paper reaching over 250,000 copies a week, members staying up day and night to lay it out, the fifteen-cents-per-paper income that kept members going, the artist Emory Douglas whose relevant artwork people loved, and the difficulty finding printers after the system warned them off.

chapters.part: Vital Organ: The Black Panther Newspaper
chapters.title: A Little Hippie Guy in Berkeley
chapters.summary: Howard tells how Max Scherr, who ran a Berkeley paper, let the Panthers print on his presses, a solidarity across groups, until larger printers, seeing the money and fearing bad publicity, also took the work despite FBI pressure, and how the government leaned on airlines to make shipping the paper costly even as distribution spread.

chapters.title: Organize the Asian Community
chapters.summary: Howard follows up on Richard Aoki, who helped with the ten-point program and was asked to organize the Asian community, working with the Red Guard Party in Berkeley, while the party built coalitions with the Filipino, Mexican American, Latino, and Puerto Rican communities, the Young Lords, and the American Indian Movement, drawing the FBI's anger; Aoki remained an ally to the end, giving Howard a pack of slides before his health failed.

chapters.title: An Ally to the End
chapters.summary: Howard praises Richard Aoki as a great member, ally, and writer he loved, and recounts writing a blistering critique when an author published a book calling Aoki an FBI informant, after which the author decided he would gain no benefit and dropped the claim.

chapters.title: Going to the Penitentiaries
chapters.summary: Howard explains that as Eldridge Cleaver became a popular spokesman, he handed off the paper and was selected to crisscross the country visiting penitentiaries and jails to check on jailed party members, assess legal situations, help lawyers, and organize defense committees, traveling from Denver to New Orleans and on to New Haven for the framing of Bobby Seale and Ericka Huggins.

chapters.title: Engineering Survival Programs
chapters.summary: Howard describes relying on community friends who contributed bail money, setting up free breakfast and free clothing programs to build support for incarcerated members, and dodging entrapment during the New Haven case, when all-night phone calls offered him cash, weapons, and dynamite, which he recognized as traps and refused, hanging up to get a few hours of sleep before strategizing.

chapters.title: The Invaders Become a Chapter
chapters.summary: Asked about lesser-known chapters, Howard recalls a group of Memphis college students called the Invaders whom the party advised and inducted as a chapter, building solidarity with Latino communities and the antiwar movement, using jail time as a recruiting tool, and remembering George Jackson, murdered as a nationwide prison organizer and still considered a hero by prisoners.

chapters.title: We Changed Their Attitude
chapters.summary: Howard argues the party's overlooked achievement was changing the perspective of angry young people, taking those with major issues who had been to prison and politically educating them, recruiting kids who would have caused trouble and teaching them to cook breakfast for schoolchildren and gather donations for clothing programs, channeling anger into work that benefited the community.

chapters.title: Why Can't the Government Do It
chapters.summary: Howard contends the party threatened the powers that be by exposing them, feeding 250,000 children free food daily, testing people for sickle-cell anemia, establishing free health clinics, and running the liberation school for children written off by the system, teaching relevant education while recruiting teachers fed up with educational bureaucracy.

chapters.title: You're Going, Take Roberta
chapters.summary: Howard recounts being sent with Roberta Alexander to Japan at the persistent invitation of Japanese students during a period of massive student rebellions, speaking at an enormous antiwar rally against the Vietnam War to students breaking out of a repressive system, and being followed by the Japanese version of the FBI as they traveled the provinces.

chapters.title: Not as Slick as the Americans
chapters.summary: Howard describes finding the surveillance comical compared to the Americans, hooking up with filmmaker Shinsuke Ogawa who sponsored their travel, spending about a month in the country and appearing in all the papers, before being told to go on to Sweden and Denmark to work with support committees rather than returning home.

chapters.title: The Base Commander Cut the Power
chapters.summary: Howard tells of working with support committees in Denmark and Sweden, visiting Paris and Germany to speak with GIs who had the Panther paper, and a base where the commander blacked out all the power to stop a reception, so the soldiers took them to a local village to talk most of the night about America, the party, and why Black GIs had no business fighting the enemy's war.

chapters.title: Come Home and Save Bobby
chapters.summary: Howard recounts speaking at colleges across Sweden, Denmark, and Finland until a message reached him that Bobby Seale had been extradited to New Haven on a murder charge in the death of Alex Rackley, urging him to come home at once to work on the paper and help organize Seale's defense, which he was anxious to do.

chapters.title: New Haven Could Be Ashes
chapters.summary: Howard describes the fourteen or eighteen months Bobby Seale and Ericka Huggins were imprisoned in New Haven, generating daily support with rallies drawing Daniel Berrigan and Dr. Benjamin Spock, the fear in New England that the city would explode, and his belief that Yale president Kingman Brewster and the courts, under FBI pressure, wanted the case gone to avoid mayhem near pristine New Haven.

chapters.title: Rockefeller Wasn't Having It
chapters.summary: Howard recounts a call after the New Haven trial that the Attica prison had exploded and inmates had asked the Panthers to come negotiate, traveling there with Bobby Seale to talk with attorneys while the warden hoped they could quell it, and how Governor Nelson Rockefeller, eyeing reelection on a tough-on-crime platform, refused to come or make concessions, costing lives needlessly.

chapters.title: Shooting Chickens in a Barrel
chapters.summary: Howard describes walking into the occupied Attica yard where inmates greeted him by name, the pitiful families of the guards begging him to help, the captured guards made to change clothes and sitting defeated by a fire, and the order the next day to retake the prison, when authorities opened fire and killed people like shooting chickens in a barrel, with the guards later proven not to have killed anyone.

chapters.part: Sam Napier and the Coast War
chapters.title: Keeping My Sanity
chapters.summary: Howard reflects on the relentless calls sending him from one crisis to the next, never knowing what to expect, and crediting something in his personality that let him deal with it and keep his goal in mind, as he sets up the next blow from the FBI's COINTELPRO operation, which they did not yet know by name.

chapters.part: Sam Napier and the Coast War
chapters.title: Sam Napier Is in the Morgue
chapters.summary: Howard recounts a call that Sam Napier, the party's beloved distribution manager who had no role in any military arm, had been killed and the New York distribution office set on fire, and how he went to a hotel where women and a group of terrified children were holed up, insisting they first find a safe place for the women and children before anything else.

chapters.part: Sam Napier and the Coast War
chapters.title: Driven Around in a Paddy Wagon
chapters.summary: Howard tells how, stepping out of the hotel after arranging safety for the women and children, police swarmed with guns drawn, drove him around in a paddy wagon for hours, and booked him into the Tombs, until a sympathetic African American assistant and attorney William Kunstler got him released on the condition that he leave New York and never come back, which he accepted.

chapters.part: Sam Napier and the Coast War
chapters.title: Then the Killing Began
chapters.summary: Howard describes how the press-fueled East Coast versus West Coast conflict escalated, how New York Panthers believed Sam Napier had been killed by East Coast members, and how Robert Webb, a West Coast Panther working the East Coast, was gunned down on the streets of New York in supposed retaliation, while Howard mourns Napier as a phenomenal organizer who built a dynamite distribution crew.

chapters.title: A Work-Study Program at Merritt
chapters.summary: Back from the East Coast, Howard recounts being selected by Merritt College president Dr. Young Park as an ombudsman running a work-study program with its own budget, objecting to a parole system that required a job to get released, and persuading the California adult authority to release parolees into the college, enrolling those without diplomas in GED and work-study tracks and others toward four-year degrees.

chapters.title: They Knocked Him on His Behind
chapters.summary: Howard describes how parolees in the program received stipends, campus meal tickets, and light library duty in exchange for keeping up their studies and attendance, until an instructor critical of the party provoked Panthers in his class to knock him down, an assault on a county employee that ended the program after a successful run.

chapters.title: Ramps and Rent Strikes
chapters.summary: Howard recalls attending endless meetings, including one with people in wheelchairs who threatened to block traffic until sidewalk curb cuts and ramps were installed on city buildings, a Panther involvement few credit, and organizing rent strikes that forced landlords to remove lead-based paint that was sickening children.

chapters.title: Close Down the Chapters
chapters.summary: Howard explains his decision to leave the party amid political differences, criticizing Huey Newton's order to disband the chapters nationwide and pull organizers into Oakland under the guise of the Bobby Seale for Mayor campaign, which Howard saw as a disservice to people who had worked their tails off to build community programs, after which members began to leave and even Bobby Seale departed without explanation.

chapters.title: I Fulfilled My Mission
chapters.summary: Howard describes the party limping along under last-minute hangers-on using its name and resources after the top-down dissolution, deciding it was time to go, and turning down a small group of organizers who offered to follow him, telling them instead to leave Oakland and use their talents wherever they wanted, with the promise he would help if ever needed.

chapters.title: A Job at Kmart
chapters.summary: Howard recounts going into retail without anyone knowing his Panther background, taking a job as a Kmart manager for about thirteen years, training in a Virginia town and being moved around, and using the role to mentor young workers, telling them to hang on to their jobs and not fly off the handle while he helped them where he could.

chapters.title: Sidestep the Snakes
chapters.summary: Howard tells how, tired of a Kmart assignment in North Carolina, he moved to the gift-oriented retailer Service Merchandise, rising from trainee to running his own stores, before management practices that fired well-paid people to do more with less while bosses took big bonuses led him to give it up as his Social Security drew near.

chapters.title: I Thought You Were Dead
chapters.summary: Howard describes how Billy X Jennings kept after him to attend reunions until he finally went to one in Washington, where old comrades thought he had died, beginning to realize he had a legacy, and recalls FBI agents who questioned his elderly aunt and handed him a Freedom of Information Act packet of accumulated surveillance with names blacked out.

chapters.title: I Kept Her Under Wraps
chapters.summary: Howard explains that he kept his Panther past hidden even from his retail coworkers and from his daughter, born in 1980, because he believed the government would harass her to get to him, telling her nothing until she was sixteen or seventeen, when he relented at a Washington reunion and began sharing old stories so no one could harm her over what he had done.

chapters.title: The Legacy Follows Me
chapters.summary: Howard describes the tremendous feedback he now receives, how the legacy draws him back into the same issues he addressed before, returning to California where he reconnected with Carol, a longtime nurse, was treated for prostate cancer, and found that combined state, county, and local forces were killing people, pulling him into the work once again.

chapters.title: A Help Line Called PACH
chapters.summary: Howard recounts holding big meetings on police violence and the ICE immigration problem, and out of those workshops creating a help line called PACH, a twenty-four-hour telephone line where people abused by police could file reports logged into a database that could later supply information for lawsuits against the police.

chapters.title: They Shot Andy Lopez in the Back
chapters.summary: Howard describes the killing of thirteen-year-old Andy Lopez, shot in the back about seven or eight times by a sheriff's deputy who claimed his toy weapon looked like an assault rifle while the boy walked his own neighborhood in daylight, an outrage that sparked demonstrations, made worse when the deputy was not indicted and was returned to the street.

chapters.title: I Got to Keep Fighting
chapters.summary: Howard closes by vowing to keep challenging the district attorney and county prosecutor and giving voice to the people through an endless list of killings, observing that some in the civil rights movement could quit and find peace while he is not so blessed and must keep fighting, before David Cline thanks him and ends the interview.

loc_video.caption: Elbert "Big Man" Howard oral history interview conducted by David P. Cline in Santa Rosa, California, 2016 June 30
